meta.title: Cart badge and cart page drift out of sync
meta.summary: The header badge and the cart page stop agreeing because one feature quietly creates a second CartService instance.
signals: The header shows 3 items while the cart page shows 0
Refreshing the cart page briefly 'fixes' only one side
The feature component has its own CartService provider

context.symptom: A user adds products from the catalog and sees the header badge move to 3, but when they open the cart page it says the cart is empty. Sometimes the cart page then adds an item and the badge still stays at 3.
context.userImpact: Users stop trusting checkout because the app cannot agree on what is actually in the cart.
context.environment: Angular storefront. `CartService` uses `providedIn: 'root'`, but the cart feature shell also lists `providers: [CartService]`. The header badge and the cart page both inject the service.

evidence.title: Visible mismatch
evidence.body: The header and the cart page can both be open at the same time, and they show different item counts even though they are supposed to share one source of truth.

evidence.title: Runtime clue
evidence.body: A debug log prints two different `CartService` instance ids: one in the app shell and one inside the cart feature.

evidence.title: Cart feature shell

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: Why are the badge and cart page out of sync?
stages.helperText: Pick the explanation that fits Angular DI, not just generic state bugs.

options.label: The cart stream is probably racing itself, so the header and page render different values from the same service.
options.feedback: A race can cause flicker, but the stronger clue here is that the two views are reading from different service instances.

options.label: The cart feature creates its own CartService instance, so that branch stops sharing state with the root instance.
options.feedback: Correct. Angular DI is hierarchical, and the component-level provider shadows the root service for that subtree.

options.label: The page is probably missing a manual `detectChanges()`, so the right cart state never reaches the template.
options.feedback: Change detection can hide symptoms, but it does not explain two different service instance ids.

options.label: `providedIn: 'root'` always guarantees one instance everywhere, so DI cannot be the issue.
options.feedback: That is the trap. Re-providing the same service in a component or route can still create another instance.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove whether the app has one shared cart service or more than one.

candidates.label: Log the CartService instance identity in the header and in the cart feature.
candidates.description: This proves whether the two screens share the same service.

candidates.label: Inspect route, feature, and component providers to see where CartService is re-provided.
candidates.description: You need to know which injector branch owns the duplicate instance.

candidates.label: Reproduce the mismatch by adding an item in one screen and reading the count in the other without a full reload.
candidates.description: Confirm it is really a shared-state split, not a one-off timing issue.

candidates.label: Replace the whole cart flow with NgRx before confirming the service scope problem.
Scatter `detectChanges()` calls until both screens appear to match.
candidates.description: Masks the symptom without proving the root cause.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes belong in the actual fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the fixes that restore one clear source of truth.

options.label: Remove the feature-level `providers: [CartService]` so the cart feature uses the root instance.
options.feedback: Correct. If the cart should be shared app-wide, the duplicate provider has to go.

options.label: Keep both service instances and manually copy cart data between them on navigation.
options.feedback: That bakes the bug into the architecture instead of fixing the scope mistake.

options.label: If a local cart really is intended, rename and scope it clearly so it is not mistaken for the app-wide cart service.
options.feedback: Correct. Explicit scope is fine; accidental shadowing is not.

options.label: Document which state is global and which services are allowed to be feature-scoped.
options.feedback: Correct. This helps prevent the same DI bug from coming back in another feature.

options.label: Refresh the cart page after every add-to-cart action so both views re-read their state.
options.feedback: That hides the mismatch but leaves the duplicate instance problem in place.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best guardrail for this bug?
stages.helperText: Pick the check that would catch a split service instance early.

options.label: Add a cross-screen test that adds to cart in one place and verifies the same count is visible everywhere without a reload.
options.feedback: Correct. This checks the user-facing contract that both screens must share the same cart state.

options.label: Track page performance because faster pages are less likely to drift out of sync.
options.feedback: Performance metrics do not guard service scope or shared-state correctness.

options.label: Have QA add one item during release smoke tests and trust that the rest of the flow is fine.
options.feedback: Manual smoke tests help, but they are too weak as the main guardrail.

options.label: Ban component and route providers completely so this can never happen again.
options.feedback: Feature-scoped providers are sometimes correct. The guardrail should check intent, not ban a useful tool.

scoreBands.label: You noticed the mismatch, but not the injector boundary
scoreBands.summary: The cart bug looked like shared state, but the real problem was still service scope.

scoreBands.label: You were close, but one fix still treated the symptom
scoreBands.summary: You connected the issue to DI, but part of the answer still leaned on refreshes or other workarounds.

scoreBands.label: You diagnosed the duplicate service instance clearly
scoreBands.summary: You proved the service split, fixed the provider scope, and protected the shared-state contract.

idealRunbook: Prove whether both screens use the same CartService instance.
Inspect where CartService is provided and which injector branch shadows the root service.
Remove the accidental duplicate provider or scope it intentionally with a different contract.
Protect the fix with a cross-screen shared-state test.

teachingBlocks.title: Angular shortcut
teachingBlocks.text: When two parts of an Angular app disagree on shared state, ask early whether they are really using the same service instance.

teachingBlocks.title: Strong answer signals
items: You mention hierarchical DI, not just generic state bugs.
You prove the duplicate instance before changing architecture.
You restore one clear source of truth instead of syncing two copies.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: When would a component-level provider actually be the right choice instead of a bug?
